meta.name: MSPSS Perceived Social Support Scale
meta.fullName: Multidimensional Scale of Perceived Social Support
meta.description: The MSPSS is a 12-item self-report measure of perceived social support from three sources: family, friends, and a significant other.
meta.author: Zimet et al. (1988)
meta.reference: Zimet GD, Dahlem NW, Zimet SG, Farley GK. The Multidimensional Scale of Perceived Social Support. J Pers Assess. 1988;52(1):30-41.
meta.instruction: Please read each statement carefully and indicate how you feel about each.

questions.text: There is a special person who is around when I am in need
There is a special person with whom I can share my joys and sorrows
My family really tries to help me
I get the emotional help and support I need from my family
I have a special person who is a real source of comfort to me
My friends really try to help me
I can count on my friends when things go wrong
I can talk about my problems with my family
I have friends with whom I can share my joys and sorrows
There is a special person in my life who cares about my feelings
My family is willing to help me make decisions
I can talk about my problems with my friends

ranges.label: Low Perceived Social Support
ranges.description: Your score suggests you perceive low levels of social support from family, friends, and significant others. Perceived lack of social support is a significant risk factor for depression, anxiety, and other mental health difficulties. Even if support is available, feeling unsupported can be very distressing.
suggestions: Consider reaching out to a counselor or therapist who can help strengthen your support network
Identify one or two people you might feel comfortable reaching out to more
Community groups, volunteer activities, or support groups can help build new connections
If you are feeling isolated, a mental health professional can help develop strategies for building social connections

ranges.label: Moderate Perceived Social Support
ranges.description: Your score indicates a moderate level of perceived social support. You have some supportive relationships but may feel that support is inconsistent or insufficient in certain areas. Strengthening these connections can enhance your resilience and well-being.
suggestions: Identify which sources of support (family, friends, significant other) feel strongest and which could be strengthened
Practice expressing your needs to people in your life; others may not realize you need more support
Invest time in nurturing your existing relationships
Consider expanding your social network through shared activities or community involvement

ranges.label: High Perceived Social Support
ranges.description: Your score suggests you perceive high levels of social support from family, friends, and/or a significant other. Strong perceived social support is a powerful protective factor for mental health and is associated with greater resilience, better coping, and higher life satisfaction.
suggestions: Continue nurturing the relationships that provide you with support
Express gratitude to the people who support you
Consider being a source of support for others in return

significant_other.label: Low
significant_other.description: Perceived significant other support is low.

significant_other.label: Moderate
significant_other.description: Perceived significant other support is moderate.

significant_other.label: High
significant_other.description: Perceived significant other support is high.
